claim: The 70.1% hapax ratio is substantially artifactual: stripping word-final glyphs drawn from the set {y, n, l, r, o} normalizes words to a stem form, reducing the unique vocabulary to roughly 30-40% of current count and the hapax ratio to below 45%, consistent with a systematic suffixation or abbreviation cipher.
evidence_for: Word-final glyph constraints are already believed [0.624] to reflect cipher structure rather than grammar
Top-frequency words daiin, aiin differ only in a leading glyph, suggesting a stem 'ain' with prefixation/suffixation
If final {y,n,l,r,o} are suffix markers, removing them should collapse many near-duplicate surface forms
High hapax ratio is a known artifact of morphologically rich languages or systematic affixation patterns
evidence_against: Some word-final variation may encode genuine semantic distinctions rather than inflectional endings
Normalizing by stripping only word-final glyphs may be too coarse â€” cipher suffixes could be multi-glyph
The recipes section has the largest word count (11,611) and second-highest entropy (3.8586), which could reflect genuine lexical richness rather than suffix inflation
test: Strip all word-final glyphs in {y, n, l, r, o} from the full corpus to produce a stemmed vocabulary. Compute: (1) new unique word count, (2) new hapax count and ratio, (3) new glyph entropy on stemmed forms. Repeat for each section separately.
test_metric: Hapax ratio of stemmed corpus vs original (70.1%), and unique word count ratio stemmed/original
test_threshold: If stemmed hapax ratio drops below 45% and unique word count drops by more than 35%, systematic suffixation is confirmed; if hapax ratio remains above 60% after stemming, suffix-stripping is not the primary driver

tests_run.test: strip all word-final glyphs in {y, n, l, r, o} from the full corpus to produce a stemmed vocabulary.
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: This test is cheap to run, directly targets the most statistically anomalous feature of the corpus (the hapax ratio), and would immediately narrow the solution space by confirming or denying whether the surface vocabulary is inflated by a mechanical suffix rule.
